title.zh: 怪水中鸟首蛇尾的异龟
title.en: Bird-Headed, Serpent-Tailed Turtle of the Guai River

summary.zh: 杻阳山怪水中的龟，鸟的头、毒蛇的尾，叫声像劈开木头；佩戴它可以不聋，还能治脚底的老茧。
summary.en: A turtle of the Guai River below Mount Niuyang, with a bird's head and a viper's tail, its call like wood splitting; worn on the body it guards against deafness and cures calluses.

description.zh:
怪水发源于杻阳之山，向东流入宪翼之水，水中多玄龟。《南山经》写它"其状如龟而鸟首虺尾，其名曰旋龟，其音如判木，佩之不聋，可以为底"。虺是毒蛇——这只龟顶着鸟喙，拖着蛇尾，叫起来像斧头劈开木头的脆响。"佩之不聋"是说带着它可保听力；"可以为底"，旧注谓底同胝，指它能治脚底的老茧。

旋龟在书中不止一见：《中山经》密山一带的豪水中也有旋龟，"鸟首而鳖尾，其音如判木"，形貌与南山的大同小异，可见这是一类广布于想象水系的生物。后世讲大禹治水的传说时，王嘉《拾遗记》有"黄龙曳尾于前，玄龟负青泥于后"之语，驮泥的玄龟常被读者与《南山经》里"多玄龟"的怪水联想到一处，鸟首蛇尾的旋龟由此也进入了治水故事的图像传统。
description.en:
The Guai River rises on Mount Niuyang and flows east into the Xianyi, and its waters teem with dark turtles. The Classic of the Southern Mountains names them the xuangui: a turtle-shaped creature with the head of a bird and the tail of a hui, a venomous snake, whose cry sounds like timber being split. The entry closes with two uses — worn on the body it keeps the ears from going deaf, and it can treat zhi, which early commentators read as the hard calluses of the foot. A beaked, serpent-tailed reptile thus doubles as a hearing charm and a foot remedy.

The creature is not confined to the south: the Classic of the Central Mountains reports xuangui in the Hao River near Mount Mi, bird-headed and soft-shell-tailed, with the same wood-splitting cry, suggesting a whole family of such turtles in the imagined waterways. Later lore drew them toward the flood legends: Wang Jia's Shiyiji pictures Yu the Great taming the waters with a yellow dragon dragging its tail ahead of him and a dark turtle hauling green clay behind, and readers have long linked that burden-bearing turtle with the dark turtles of the Guai River, folding the xuangui into the iconography of the flood.
